entities.description: A manually keyed electronic selection from Morris's 1862 edition. The represented source layers are the title page; fourteen-line prologue; table of contents; complete verse text; index of words and dishes; final editorial note; and printing colophon.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00146

rights.basis: The U-M item and collection apply No Copyright - United States to public CME content. This is a rights statement, not a license, and institutional service terms may still apply.
rights.component: U-M electronic text and represented public-domain source material

entities.description: An anonymous Middle English culinary recipe collection in rhyming couplets. DIMEV v1.0 describes 127 recipes in 1,429 lines plus a fourteen-line prologue. Its exact composition date and place remain insecure; the full Sloane witness is fifteenth century, and Pepys MS 1047 transmits four later-fifteenth-century excerpts.
entities.label: Liber Cure Cocorum
notes: Morris's North-West Lancashire claim is an 1862 linguistic inference; DIMEV's LALME/eLALME localization to Cheshire describes the Sloane witness language and is not treated as proof of the work's composition place.
No consulted primary or scholarly catalog source names the author.

bibliographic.citation: Richard Morris, ed., Liber Cure Cocorum, Copied and Edited from the Sloane MS. 1986 (Berlin: published for the Philological Society by A. Asher & Co., 1862), iv + 61 pp.
bibliographic.publisher: A. Asher & Co., for the Philological Society
bibliographic.title: Liber Cure Cocorum

entities.description: The first printed edition of the poem, with source title page, two-page preface, fourteen-line prologue, table of contents, verse text, footnotes, index of words and dishes, final editorial note, and colophon. Morris says it was printed from a transcript of Sloane MS 1986, takes responsibility for bracketed words, and says textual alterations are acknowledged in footnotes.
entities.label: Richard Morris, Liber Cure Cocorum (1862)
notes: The title-page phrase copied and edited from the manuscript does not override the preface's more precise statement that the edition was printed from a transcript.
The edition's preface occupies PDF pp. 9-10 in the cached Internet Archive file and is not encoded in CME00146.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1862 and predates the current United States public-domain cutoff by many decades. This does not determine provider terms for a modern digital reproduction.
rights.component: Morris's 1862 edition, typography, notes, index, and underlying medieval text; later digital reproduction excluded

PDF pp. 9-10 = Morris preface, retained in cache but omitted from CME00146

Complete provider-derived OCR for the Morris scan
notes: OCR is a complete provider derivative, not a substitute for the checked page images or XML transcription.

rights.component: Internet Archive digital reproduction of the University of Leeds copy

entities.description: A 96-page complete PDF scan of the University of Leeds copy, including bound-volume carrier matter and the complete Morris tract. The encoded title page is PDF p. 7; the encoded prologue through colophon is PDF pp. 11-71.
entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Morris's 1862 Liber Cure Cocorum

rights.basis: The exact Internet Archive item metadata links Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0. The mark reports no known copyright but is not a license; platform and source-institution terms remain distinct.
rights.component: Internet Archive digital reproduction of the University of Leeds copy

entities.description: The modern working transcript from which Morris says the 1862 edition was first printed. It is not the medieval codex. Morris does not identify its maker, date, location, survival, or whether it was copied directly from the original manuscript.
entities.label: Unidentified working transcript of Sloane MS 1986 used by Morris
notes: Typed as manuscript_witness because lineage schema 1.0.0 has no dedicated transcript-intermediary entity type.
No creator_id is assigned: the title page shows Morris's editorial responsibility, but the preface does not establish that he personally made the transcript.

Publication or further reproduction permission is separate from private-study image supply.

access.format: Complete public digital facsimile not located
notes: The exact current record reports Digitised Content as No and supplies no digital-object URL. This is a dated negative finding for the routes checked, not proof that no private, internal, microfilm, or future surrogate exists.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century parchment quarto of 110 folios containing Middle English and Latin texts. The Boke of Curtasye occupies ff. 12r-27r, and the full Liber Cure Cocorum follows at ff. 27r-56v; its fourteen-line prologue begins on f. 27r, its table follows, and its principal recipe text begins on f. 29r.
entities.label: British Library, Sloane MS 1986

physicalDescription.extent: 110 folios; Liber Cure Cocorum at ff. 27r-56v
notes: The current BL record describes the work as English verse and gives the terminal folio as 56b.
DIMEV v1.0 localizes this manuscript's language as LALME/eLALME LP 43, grid 406 400, Cheshire; that witness-level localization is not a composition-place assertion.

rights.basis: The anonymous text is embodied in a fifteenth-century manuscript and predates modern copyright by centuries. British Library access and image-supply terms remain separate.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Liber Cure Cocorum text in Sloane MS 1986; physical ownership and any supplied photography excluded

rights.basis: British Library Imaging Services supplies files under purpose-specific terms, makes the customer responsible for rights clearance, and requires separate permission for publication or further reproduction. No open license for a future commissioned set is assumed.
rights.component: Any newly supplied British Library photography of Sloane MS 1986 only
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom provider and supply terms

access.format: Physical manuscript consultation subject to condition and library hours

access.accessMethod: Seek image supply and publication or reuse permission through the current Pepys Library and Cambridge University Library imaging routes
access.format: Provider images and a purpose-specific permission decision
notes: The current Magdalene page says publication images must be purchased and permission obtained. Obtain written clarification before relying on the archived manifest's CC BY-NC 3.0 wording for any public or commercial use.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Late fifteenth century, certainly in or after 1470/1471

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century paper recipe and instructional miscellany copied by one Anglicana-formata scribe. Four Liber Cure Cocorum excerpts occupy ff. 15r-16v: sauce for roast mallard, woodcock and related birds, fritters, and pot herbs. They form a partial witness, not a complete copy of the 127-recipe work.

holding.notes: The official digital record was created and served by Cambridge University Library, but custodianship is the Pepys Library at Magdalene College.

entities.label: Magdalene College, Pepys MS 1047

physicalDescription.extent: i + 23 + i leaves; Liber Cure Cocorum excerpts at ff. 15r-16v
notes: Single columns, usually twenty lines to the page, copied by a single scribe in Anglicana formata.
The lower parts of ff. 13 and 14 are cut away, and one leaf is missing after f. 13; these losses precede the target excerpt leaves.

rights.basis: The anonymous excerpts are embodied in a late-fifteenth-century manuscript and predate modern copyright by centuries. Pepys Library ownership, consultation rules, and image rights are separate.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Liber Cure Cocorum excerpts in Pepys MS 1047; physical ownership and digital images excluded

access.provider: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data and project files

entities.description: A versioned scholarly catalog record describing Liber Cure Cocorum as 127 culinary recipes in 1,429 couplet lines plus a fourteen-line prologue, the complete Sloane witness, and four excerpts in Pepys MS 1047. Separate linked records 5104, 1420, 6721, and 5106 describe those excerpts.
entities.label: DIMEV v1.0 record 3799 / IMEV 2361
notes: DIMEV's 1,429-line main-text count differs by eight from the 1,437 L elements in the XML's main poem division; the measurements are preserved separately pending a line-by-line reconciliation.
DIMEV's Pepys folio locators were refined through direct image inspection: 5104 crosses 15r-15v; 1420 crosses 15v-16r; 6721 is on 16r; and 5106 crosses 16r-16v.

rights.basis: The versioned release README and LICENSE identify Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International.
rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data and project files

next_steps: Search surviving Richard Morris and Philological Society correspondence, minutes, and papers for the 1862 project.
Ask the British Library and University of Leeds whether the transcript is cataloged with Morris papers or the Leeds source copy.
Continue to model the transcript as an unidentified intermediary unless primary evidence establishes maker, route, and survival.
openQuestions.question: Who made the working transcript used by Morris, was it copied directly from Sloane MS 1986, and does it survive?

next_steps: Submit the recorded British Library Reference Services enquiry with shelfmark Sloane MS 1986 and target ff. 27r-56v.
Ask whether an internal, legacy, or microfilm surrogate exists before commissioning new photography.
If no existing surrogate can be supplied, request a whole-object quotation and record exact image inventory, omissions, terms, and work boundaries before caching.
openQuestions.question: Does a complete public or restricted digital facsimile of Sloane MS 1986 exist outside the current British Library routes checked on 2026-07-13?

next_steps: Ask Cambridge University Library to restore or redirect the live IIIF and JSON endpoints and confirm the canonical item identifier.
Ask the Pepys Library and Cambridge University Library in writing whether noncommercial public republication may rely on the archived manifest license.
Require purpose-specific written permission before commercial or publication reuse; keep the private research cache nonpublic meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: Will CUDL restore the live Pepys MS 1047 manifest and metadata, and how should the archived CC BY-NC 3.0 download statement be reconciled with the current Pepys Library publication-permission policy?

next_steps: Collate DIMEV's counting rule against Morris printed pp. 5-55 and XML DIV1[4] line by line.
Keep the fourteen-line prologue and two-line final editorial quotation outside the main-text comparison.
Do not normalize either count silently until the eight-line difference is explained.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV v1.0 report 1,429 main-text lines while the XML main poem division contains 1,437 L elements?

next_steps: Collate the four excerpt passages diplomatically against Sloane MS 1986 once complete images or direct consultation are available.
Consult modern textual scholarship on Middle English culinary recipe circulation.
Do not assert that Pepys MS 1047 copied directly from Sloane MS 1986 or vice versa without stemmatic evidence.
openQuestions.question: What textual relationship connects the four Pepys MS 1047 excerpts to the full Sloane MS 1986 tradition?

relations.objectLabel: Gerald A. J. Hodgett, Stere Htt Well (1972)

subject.description: Pepys witness and four excerpt facsimile references plus Bibliography.xml Hodgett1972

reviewNotes: The anonymous Middle English culinary work, two manuscript witnesses, Morris's unidentified working transcript, Morris's printed edition, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML are separate entities.
Morris's title page says copied and edited from Sloane MS 1986, but his preface more precisely says first printed from a transcript; the transcript maker, directness, and survival are unresolved.
Pepys MS 1047 contains four excerpts, not a complete second copy: DIMEV records 5104, 1420, 6721, and 5106, and direct images place them across ff. 15r-16v.

Complete Pepys cache coverage means every one of the 55 canvases exposed by the archived official provider manifest, including covers and all imaged leaves; it does not assert capture of unexposed physical edges or other surfaces.

The archived CUDL manifest licenses downloadable images CC BY-NC 3.0, while the current Magdalene page says publication requires purchase and permission. Both statements are retained, and the more conservative permission route governs any proposed publication or commercial reuse.
A private, gitignored cache documents research access and fixity but does not itself grant public redistribution, publication, or commercial reuse.

lineage.summary: CME00146 is the General Corpus copy of the University of Michigan electronic text keyed from Richard Morris's 1862 Liber Cure Cocorum. Morris says that he printed the work from a transcript of British Library Sloane MS 1986, but he does not identify the transcript's maker, present location, or whether it was made directly from the codex; that intermediary is therefore modeled separately rather than silently collapsing the edition into the manuscript. The encoded selection retains the source title page, fourteen-line prologue, table of contents, complete verse recipe collection, index, final editorial note, and printing colophon, while omitting Morris's two-page preface and nontextual scan matter. Sloane MS 1986 contains the full work at ff. 27r-56v. Magdalene College Pepys MS 1047 independently preserves four excerpts at ff. 15r-16v; a complete 55-canvas CUDL image object is cached from the exact official manifest preserved by the Internet Archive on 2025-06-14 because the live manifest and metadata endpoints returned server errors during review. No verified public facsimile was found for Sloane MS 1986; physical consultation and a paid whole-object photography route are recorded. DIMEV v1.0, Morris's complete edition scan, the Pepys image object, Hodgett's controlled-lending 1972 Pepys facsimile, and the British Library rate card have complete or metadata-complete private cache records without implying redistribution rights.

catalogSummary.title: Liber Cure Cocorum

catalogingSubject.label: Liber Cure Cocorum
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English verse culinary compilation Liber Cure Cocorum, not British Library Sloane MS 1986 or Magdalene College Pepys MS 1047 as physical objects, Richard Morris's 1862 printed edition and its modern paratext, the unidentified working transcript used by Morris, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. The full work survives in Sloane MS 1986; Pepys MS 1047 preserves only four excerpts.

contentParts.summary: The 1862 title, copied-and-edited statement, editor credentials, Philological Society imprint, Berlin publisher, and date.

The 140 XML LG wrappers do not imply 140 recipes and are not substituted for DIMEV's 127-recipe intellectual count.

assertion.notes: Morris's linguistic estimate and the date of the Sloane witness do not independently date the work's composition; no exact normalized range is assigned.

dateStatements.display: Not securely established; generally associated with the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: This is a joint qualified attestation window, not a composition date. Sloane MS 1986 preserves the complete work and is dated only to the fifteenth century; Pepys MS 1047 preserves four excerpts and is dated after 1470/1471. Their ranges overlap, so the consulted evidence does not establish which surviving witness is earlier, and no singular lineage entity is asserted.

dateStatements.display: Fifteenth century: the complete Sloane MS 1986 witness and the partial Pepys MS 1047 witness have overlapping production ranges

extent.description: At work level DIMEV v1.0 reports 127 recipes, 1,429 main-text lines, and a fourteen-line prologue. At representation level the XML has seven DIV1 units and 1,453 L elements: 14 prologue lines, 1,437 main-poem lines in 140 LG wrappers, and 2 quotation lines in the final editorial note. The extent's 544 numbered items total the six content parts with integer item counts (the table remains unnumbered here), while verse_lines records all 1,453 XML L elements; only 1,451 of those lines belong to the prologue and main work. The XML also contains 128 table rows, 413 index ITEM elements, one TEMPHEAD workflow ITEM, 62 PB milestones, 75 notes, and later edition paratext. Recipe count, encoded-part item count, XML line count, page milestones, table rows, and index items are distinct measurements.

formStatements.description: A fourteen-line prologue and 127-recipe culinary text in rhyming couplets, rhyme pattern aa; the table and modern editorial paratext do not change the primary form of the work.

assertion.notes: The speaker explicitly undertakes to teach the practices of cookery, and the directions are organized in verse.
Latin is not treated as a second full-text language.

scope.description: Brief rubrics, headings, and the explicit embedded in a predominantly Middle English work

notes: The catalog summary's date deliberately avoids the frequently repeated c. 1420-1440 estimate because the consulted secure sources date the surviving witnesses more confidently than the abstract composition event.
DIMEV's LALME/eLALME LP 43, grid 406 400, Cheshire localization is retained as evidence about the Sloane witness language and is not promoted into a work-level place of composition.
Latin occurs only in brief rubrics, headings, and the explicit; modern English in Morris's index and note is an editorial layer rather than a second language of the medieval work.
The XML proofing TEMPHEAD is a repository workflow marker, not a culinary item, witness date, or source-edition date.
Morris's title-page wording does not establish that he personally made the unidentified transcript used to prepare the edition.

next_steps: Consult modern linguistic and culinary-text scholarship based directly on the Sloane text and independent recipe parallels.
Distinguish composition evidence from Morris's 1862 North-West Lancashire inference and from LALME/eLALME localization of the Sloane copy.
Do not normalize the work to c. 1430, Lancashire, or Cheshire without source-specific support.
openQuestions.question: When and where was Liber Cure Cocorum composed, as distinct from the date and Cheshire linguistic localization of its Sloane witness?

next_steps: Collate DIMEV's counting rule line by line against Morris printed pp. 5-55 and XML DIV1[4].
Keep the fourteen-line prologue and the two quotation lines in Morris's final note outside the main-text comparison.
Preserve both counts until the eight-line difference is explained.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV v1.0 report 1,429 main-text lines while the XML main poem division contains 1,437 L elements?

next_steps: Prepare diplomatic transcriptions of the four Pepys passages and collate them with Sloane once direct or complete-image access is available.
Consult modern scholarship on circulation and recombination of Middle English culinary recipes.
Avoid asserting direct witness-to-witness copying without textual and codicological evidence.
openQuestions.question: What textual relationship connects the four Pepys MS 1047 excerpts to the complete Sloane MS 1986 witness?

notes: Do not infer a specific composition place from Morris's North-West Lancashire proposal, DIMEV's Cheshire localization of the Sloane witness, current London or Cambridge custody, or the Berlin imprint.

assertion.notes: No transcriber responsibility is assigned because Morris does not identify who made the working transcript from which he printed the text.

scope.description: The represented layers of Morris's 1862 source edition

assertion.notes: The prologue organizes cookery among the hasteler, pasteler, and potagere, while the recipe collection includes service guidance for fish and flesh days.

subjects.label: Household food service and course organization

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English culinary compilation that teaches cookery in rhyming couplets. A fourteen-line prologue introduces the courtly crafts of the hasteler, pasteler, and potagere; a table precedes 127 recipes for pottages, broths, meat, poultry, fish, baked dishes, sauces, preserves, herbs, and fish- and flesh-day service.

CME00146 retains Morris's source title page, the fourteen-line prologue, table of contents, complete main verse text, index of words and dishes, final editorial note, and printing colophon. It omits Morris's two-page preface, even though that preface supplies the crucial statement that the edition was printed from an unidentified transcript of Sloane MS 1986.

DIMEV v1.0 describes 127 recipes in 1,429 main-text lines plus a fourteen-line prologue. The XML has 1,437 L elements in the main poem division, fourteen in the prologue, and two more inside the final modern editorial note. The unexplained eight-line difference between DIMEV and the main XML division is retained rather than silently normalized.

Sloane MS 1986 transmits the complete work at ff. 27r-56v. Pepys MS 1047 preserves only four excerpts at ff. 15r-16v: mallard sauce, preparation of woodcock and related birds, fritters, and pot herbs. No direct copying relationship between the two codices is asserted.

assertion.notes: The Sloane-derived explicit reads Explicit Liber cure Cocorum; DIMEV v1.0 uses the same title.

titles.supplied_by: Stable explicit, editorial, and DIMEV usage

assertion.notes: Morris calls this an appropriate English title derived from the phrase in the opening; it is not represented as a separate medieval title page.
